claim: Louis represented the departure as a purposeful attempt to recover liberty and secure himself and his family.
Louis argued that the emerging settlement denied him effective freedom and sufficient authority.
Drouet reported suspecting the travelers partly from resemblance to the king's image on an assignat.
Drouet reported first taking the announced road toward Verdun, then learning near Clermont that the carriage had taken the road toward Varennes.
Drouet reported traveling with Guillaume by side roads and reaching Varennes while the carriage was still there.
Drouet reported warning an innkeeper, who then alerted others.
Drouet reported that he and Guillaume obstructed the bridge passage with vehicles.
Drouet reported involvement by municipal officials, the National Guard, residents, and passport inspection before detention.
The municipal record describes officials, residents, National Guardsmen, alarms, posts, barricades, and reinforcements.
Drouet and the municipal record both describe vehicle obstruction at the bridge passage but disagree about the actors.
The municipal record attributes Sauce observable roles in alerting colleagues, requesting the passport, lodging the travelers, summoning Judge Destez, and arranging security.
Delays accumulated and planned escort rendezvous failed or became ineffective.
The reviewed route reconstruction preserves Drouet's reported turn from the announced Verdun road toward Varennes after receiving corrected route information.
Varennes included an approach and halt zone, an onward bridge passage, and a planned relay beyond the bridge.
The Assembly's 22 June proclamation described the royal family as having been carried off.
On 26 June William Short reported that public terminology had shifted from abduction to evasion.
A 14 July Cordeliers petition argued that confidence in an official called king was impossible and requested a republic or wider consultation.
The Constitution of 3 September 1791 still declared the government monarchical and delegated executive power to the king.
Moustier testified that Louis personally ordered advance preparation of courier clothing.
Maldent testified that Louis led him from a concealed room and assigned him behind the departing carriage.
Fersen's 20 June journal records advance timing and arrest-contingency discussion involving Louis.
France had been undergoing revolutionary political change since 1789.
Political authority was being reallocated and contested among national institutions and the king.
Louis XVI remained king in June 1791; France did not become a republic until September 1792.
The written constitution was unfinished in June 1791 and would define and limit royal power.
The royal family resided at the Tuileries in Paris.
A crowd and National Guard prevented Louis's attempted departure for Saint-Cloud on 18 April 1791.
The royal family secretly left the Tuileries during the night of 20-21 June 1791.
